title: Calibrated Neural Collapse: Controlling Within-Class Representation Geometry for Reliable Supervised Learning
description: We propose the Calibrated Collapse Regularizer (CCR), a training-time regularizer that prevents excessive neural collapse (NC1) in supervised learning by maintaining a minimum within-class feature spread. This preserves geometric information about sample-to-boundary proximity that is essential for well-calibrated predictions, while retaining the inter-class separation (NC2) needed for high accuracy.
motivation: Neural collapse drives within-class features to collapse onto their class means, producing maximally confident predictions for all samples regardless of their proximity to decision boundaries. This fundamentally destroys the geometric signal needed for calibration. While recent work has shown that controlling NC degree helps OOD detection (Harun et al., ICML 2025) and that NC relates to calibration (Guo et al., TMLR 2025), no method has been proposed to control NC specifically for calibration. Post-hoc methods like temperature scaling and Feature Clipping address symptoms rather than the root cause in representation geometry.
proposed_approach: CCR adds a hinge-style regularizer to the training loss that activates when within-class feature spread drops below a threshold: L_CCR = mean_c max(0, tau - S_c), where S_c is the average squared distance of class-c features from their running class prototype. An adaptive variant sets tau proportional to the minimum inter-class distance, requiring no dataset-specific tuning. A spectral extension (CCR-S) additionally controls the effective dimensionality of within-class features. The approach has negligible computational overhead (only requires maintaining running class prototypes) and can be combined with any supervised training objective. We also provide theoretical analysis under Gaussian feature assumptions showing that the calibration-optimal within-class variance is neither zero (full collapse) nor infinite, motivating the partial-collapse approach.

related_work.title: Prevalence of neural collapse during the terminal phase of deep learning training
related_work.relevance: Foundational paper identifying neural collapse (NC1-NC4). We build on this framework but argue full NC1 is harmful for calibration.

related_work.title: Controlling Neural Collapse Enhances Out-of-Distribution Detection and Transfer Learning
related_work.relevance: Most closely related — controls NC degree via entropy regularization, but targets OOD detection, not calibration. We use a different regularizer (variance floor vs. entropy) and different objective.

related_work.title: Cross Entropy versus Label Smoothing: A Neural Collapse Perspective
related_work.relevance: Analyzes NC-calibration connection, finding excessive NC1 causes overconfidence. Analytical only — does not propose a new training method.

related_work.title: Balancing Two Classifiers via A Simplex ETF Structure for Model Calibration
related_work.relevance: Uses ETF structure for calibration but at classifier level, not representation level. Our approach modifies the learned representations directly.

related_work.title: Feature Clipping for Uncertainty Calibration
related_work.relevance: Post-hoc method observing that high-ECE samples have larger feature variance. Supports our hypothesis but provides no training-time solution or theory.

related_work.title: Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.authors: Khosla, Teterwak, Wang, Sarna, Tian, Isola, Maschinot, Liu, Krishnan
related_work.relevance: Seminal supervised representation learning method. CCR can be applied on top of SupCon to improve calibration of contrastive representations.

related_work.title: On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks
related_work.relevance: Foundational calibration paper establishing that modern DNNs are overconfident and proposing temperature scaling. Our method addresses the root representation-level cause.

related_work.title: Impact of Regularization on Calibration and Robustness: From the Representation Space Perspective
related_work.relevance: Analyzes how existing regularizers affect calibration through representations. Explanatory work that motivates our explicit representation-level intervention.

related_work.title: Quantifying the Variability Collapse of Neural Networks
related_work.relevance: Proposes VCI metric for NC1. Connected to our measurement of collapse degree but focuses on transfer learning, not calibration.

related_work.title: Variational Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.relevance: State-of-the-art supervised contrastive method with adaptive temperature. CCR can be combined with VarCon for calibrated contrastive representations.

hypothesis: There exists a calibration-optimal degree of neural collapse — specifically a non-zero within-class covariance — that maintains high classification accuracy while significantly improving expected calibration error. The Calibrated Collapse Regularizer (CCR) can target this optimum by selectively preventing NC1 collapse while preserving NC2 structure, achieving Pareto-dominant accuracy-calibration trade-offs compared to existing regularization methods (label smoothing, Mixup) and complementing post-hoc calibration (temperature scaling).

primary: CCR reduces ECE by >=20% relative to standard CE on at least 2/3 datasets with accuracy loss <=1.5%
CCR demonstrates selective NC1 control: NC1 metric measurably higher under CCR while NC2 remains within 10% of fully collapsed value
secondary: CCR Pareto frontier dominates label smoothing and Mixup on accuracy-vs-ECE on at least 2/3 datasets
CCR + temperature scaling outperforms temperature scaling alone
Theoretical ECE-NC1 relationship empirically validated (rank correlation across hyperparameter sweep)
refutation_conditions: NC1 degree does not significantly correlate with ECE (less than 0.3 rank correlation)
Post-hoc temperature scaling on standard models matches or beats CCR without accuracy cost

compute_requirements.feasibility_note: Each CIFAR training run takes ~12-15 min on A6000. Main results require ~24 runs (6 hrs). Pareto sweep adds ~2 hrs. Analysis uses saved checkpoints.
